summary: The character Manon Lescaut titles two major operas: Jules Massenet's Manon (1884) and Giacomo Puccini's Manon Lescaut (1893), both based on the Abbé Prévost novel. Manon is a young woman torn between love (for Des Grieux) and a life of luxury; she dies in Des Grieux's arms, in the Massenet opera while being deported, in the Puccini on the outskirts of New Orleans.

works.name: Manon (Massenet) – The Work as a Whole
works.description: French opera (1884) by Jules Massenet, based on the Abbé Prévost novel. Centers on the character Manon Lescaut, torn between love for Des Grieux and a life of luxury. She sings 'Adieu, notre petite table' when she decides to leave for Monsieur de Bretigny, and later dies in Des Grieux's arms. Massenet dismissed Puccini's opera on the same subject as full of 'powder and minuets.' The opera was adapted into a 1974 ballet by Kenneth MacMillan (L'histoire de Manon). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Manon sings 'Adieu, notre petite table' when she decides to leave Des Grieux (to go with Monsieur de Bretigny)
examples: This character sings the seduction aria 'N'est-ce plus ma main' before being arrested for cheating at gambling in the following act.
A protagonist sings 'Adieu, notre petite table' when she decides to go with Monsieur de Bretigny.

clues.clue: Manon dies in the arms of her lover Des Grieux; based on the Abbé Prévost novel
examples: That protagonist of an opera by this composer dies in the arms of Chevalier des Grieux and was based on a novel by the Abbe Prevost.
This French opera was based on a novel by the Abbe Prevost.

clues.clue: The character also sings the seduction aria 'N'est-ce plus ma main' before being arrested for cheating at gambling
examples: In a different opera, this soprano character sings the seduction aria 'N'est-ce plus ma main' before being arrested for cheating at gambling in the following act.

clues.clue: Carmen is seen as a precursor to Manon (the opera character)
examples: Carmen is seen as a precursor to such characters as the title woman of this opera, who sings 'Adieu, notre petite table.'

clues.clue: A 1974 ballet by Kenneth MacMillan (L'histoire de Manon) uses Massenet's music without drawing from his opera; the 'Elegie' underscores the pas de deux between Manon and Des Grieux
examples: A 1974 ballet about this character uses music by Jules Massenet without drawing from his opera titled for her.
L'histoire de Manon was created by this choreographer best known for his 1965 reimagining of Romeo and Juliet for the Royal Ballet.

clues.clue: Massenet's Manon ends with her death while being deported (marching to Le Havre); Puccini's ends with her death near New Orleans
examples: The title character of one of his operas dies in her lover's arms while marching to Le Havre to be deported.
This character dies in a Louisiana desert in an Italian opera based on a novel by Abbe Prevost.

works.name: Manon Lescaut (Puccini) – The Work as a Whole
works.description: Italian opera (1893) by Giacomo Puccini, also based on the Abbé Prévost novel. Puccini dismissed Massenet's opera as full of 'powder and minuets.' The title character dies on the outskirts of New Orleans in a desert. The opera includes an intermezzo between Acts 2 and 3 representing the protagonist's journey to Le Havre; the finale draws on a string quartet Puccini composed for the funeral of the Duke of Aosta. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The title character dies near New Orleans / in a Louisiana desert in the arms of Des Grieux
examples: This character dies in a Louisiana desert in an Italian opera based on a novel by Abbe Prevost.

In this Puccini opera, the title character leaves Des Grieux to live with Geronte, but is later sent to New Orleans after changing her mind again. She dies in the arms of Des Grieux while trying to cross a desert without any water.

clues.clue: Act 1 tenor aria 'Donna non vidi mai' — Des Grieux sings of his infatuation upon meeting Manon
examples: A man tells this character, 'I have never seen a woman such as this,' or 'Donna non vidi mai,' after learning that she is headed for a convent.
In act 1 of this opera, the male lead sings of his infatuation in 'Donna non vidi mai.'

clues.clue: Act 2 aria 'In quelle trine morbide' — Manon expresses frustration over how boring her current lover (Geronte) is
examples: The title character of this opera expresses frustration over how boring her current lover is in the aria 'In quelle trine morbide.'

clues.clue: Closing aria 'Sola, perduta, abbandonata' ('lonely, lost, and abandoned') sung as Manon nears death
examples: Shortly after her lover runs away, this character mourns her 'belta funesta,' or 'fatal beauty,' after declaring herself to be 'lonely, lost, and abandoned' in a closing aria.
The protagonist of this opera sings the aria 'Sola, perduta, abbandonata' as she nears death.

clues.clue: The opera has an intermezzo between Acts 2 and 3, representing the protagonist's journey to Le Havre; descriptions of a character's thoughts appear in the score during this intermezzo
examples: Descriptions of one character's thoughts appear in the score during this opera's intermezzo, which divides acts 2 and 3 and the protagonist's journey to Le Havre.

clues.clue: The finale draws on a string quartet Puccini composed for the funeral of the Duke of Aosta
examples: Manon Lescaut's finale draws on a string quartet named for these objects composed by Giacomo Puccini for the funeral of the Duke of Aosta.

clues.clue: Puccini dismissed Massenet's opera on the same subject as full of 'powder and minuets,' despite including his own minuet in Act 2
examples: The composer of this opera dismissed a similar work as full of 'powder and minuets,' in contrast to his own Italian passion, despite including a minuet of his own in the second act of this opera.

clues.clue: Title character dies while trying to cross a desert (without water) on the outskirts of New Orleans
examples: She dies in the arms of Des Grieux while trying to cross a desert without any water.

comprehensive_summary:
The character Manon Lescaut, from the Abbé Prévost novel, titles two major operas: Jules Massenet's Manon (1884) and Giacomo Puccini's Manon Lescaut (1893). Both center on the same character: a young French woman torn between love for the Chevalier Des Grieux and a life of luxury and comfort, who ultimately dies in Des Grieux's arms.

In Massenet's opera, Manon sings the celebrated aria 'Adieu, notre petite table' when she chooses to leave Des Grieux for the wealthier Monsieur de Bretigny. Later she also sings the seduction aria 'N'est-ce plus ma main' before being arrested for cheating at gambling. The opera ends with Manon dying in Des Grieux's arms while being marched to Le Havre for deportation. Carmen is considered a precursor to the character type that Manon represents. In 1974, choreographer Kenneth MacMillan created the ballet L'histoire de Manon using Massenet's music but not drawing from the opera itself; Massenet's 'Elegie' underscores the pas de deux between Manon and Des Grieux in that ballet.

Puccini's Manon Lescaut has become the more frequently clued work. Puccini famously dismissed Massenet's opera on the same subject as full of 'powder and minuets,' while boasting of his own Italian passion — though he included a minuet of his own in Act 2. In Act 1, Des Grieux sings the aria 'Donna non vidi mai' ('I have never seen a woman such as this') upon meeting Manon, who is heading to a convent. In Act 2, Manon sings 'In quelle trine morbide,' expressing frustration over how boring her current lover Geronte is. The opera includes an intermezzo between Acts 2 and 3 representing the protagonist's journey to Le Havre, with descriptions of a character's thoughts written into the score. The title character dies in the desert on the outskirts of New Orleans, singing the aria 'Sola, perduta, abbandonata' ('lonely, lost, and abandoned') as she nears death. The opera's finale draws on a string quartet Puccini composed for the funeral of the Duke of Aosta.

recursive_suggestions: Jules Massenet — French composer with many clued operas including Manon, Werther, Thais; merits its own analysis (search: 'Massenet', category: Fine Arts)
Giacomo Puccini — Italian composer; Manon Lescaut is his first major opera success (search: 'Puccini', category: Fine Arts)
